title: compute_all_metrics return shape is unchanged by the sweep — but the failure-soft contract changes the OBSERVABLE shape of metrics_json: keys are now 'missing' vs 'set to None'
summary: Pre-sweep: each try block was `metrics_json[k] = X; except: pass`. On failure, the key was ABSENT from metrics_json. Post-sweep: same — the key is absent because the assignment never completed. Type-wise the dict shape is unchanged (Dict[str, Any] where any key may be absent). BUT: the test parametrize asserts `scalar_key not in mj or mj[scalar_key] is None`. The 'is None' clause is unreachable today (the dict is never assigned None), so the test is permissive of a future refactor that DOES set None. That's defensive; not a bug. Annotation isn't drifted because compute_all_metrics has no per-key type contract — the dict has been heterogeneous since pre-sweep. Net: no type-design regression.
evidence: metrics.py:325-447 try blocks never assign None on failure; they leave the key absent. test_metrics.py:991-993 accepts BOTH 'absent' and 'None' shapes — over-permissive but not wrong.
fix: None required. Optional follow-up: tighten the parametrized test assertion to `assert scalar_key not in mj` (drop the `or is None` clause), pinning the current absent-key contract so a future None-assignment regression is caught.

title: fetch_daily_pnl return type `list[dict[str, Any]]` unchanged; fail-loud transformation preserves it
summary: The signature `-> list[dict[str, Any]]` is preserved. The inner Bybit branch's new WARNING does not re-raise, so the function continues to return `daily_pnl` (possibly empty). The outer wrapper at L620 also returns `daily_pnl`. Type-wise no drift. Annotation matches behavior.

title: `returns_len_for_log: int | None` is the implicit type — but the helper is only used inside the function where `returns: pd.Series` is non-Optional
summary: `returns_len_for_log = len(returns) if returns is not None else None` has implicit type `int | None`. But returns is typed `pd.Series` (non-Optional) — the None branch is unreachable. The variable's type narrowing is correct but unnecessarily wide. mypy/pyright won't complain because the type widens harmlessly. Documented as code-reviewer LOW; this is the type-design lens.

title: Three incompatible failure-mode encodings coexist in metrics_json — `*_status` discriminator (r_squared), `*_error` companion string (3 sites), and absent-key (11 inline scalars) — type design fails to express the pattern
summary: The sweep + the pre-existing r_squared work establish THREE non-interchangeable encodings for the same semantic ('value vs failure' for an optional scalar/series): (1) `r_squared` companion `r_squared_status: 'no_benchmark' | 'ok' | 'error'` enum, (2) `drawdown_episodes_error` / `benchmark_metrics_error` / `benchmark_returns_error` raw `str(exc)[:200]` blob, (3) the 11 new inline qstats scalars where failure leaves the KEY ABSENT with no companion. A consumer trying to distinguish 'computation failed' from 'insufficient data' from 'not requested' must check three different shapes depending on which scalar it reads. The type contract is therefore fictional: `metrics_json: dict[str, Any]` says nothing about which keys carry their own error sidecar. The sweep CHOSE to add `*_error` to drawdown_episodes / benchmark_metrics / benchmark_returns but NOT to var_1d_95 / cvar / gini / omega / gain_pain / tail_ratio / smart_sharpe / smart_sortino / profit_factor / skewness / kurtosis / var_1m_99 / outlier_*. The 11 latter sites collapse the same three-state semantics the docstring at L335-345 explicitly identifies. This is the exact invariant-expression defect the type-design framework calls out: 'invariants enforced through documentation' (the docstring lists three states the code can no longer represent).
evidence: metrics.py:556 `drawdown_episodes_error = str(exc)[:200]`. metrics.py:606 `benchmark_metrics_error = str(exc)[:200]`. metrics.py:636 `benchmark_returns_error = str(exc)[:200]`. metrics.py:836 `r_squared_status = 'no_benchmark'` (+ L854/856). metrics.py:325-447 — 11 scalar try blocks log WARNING but write nothing to metrics_json on failure. The contract is provably non-uniform.
fix: Pick ONE encoding and apply it consistently. Cheapest surgical option: add a single top-level `metrics_json['computation_errors']: dict[str, str]` map that the WARNING sites populate (one map of scalar_name → str(exc)[:200]). Consumers grep ONE key. Bigger fix: graduate `metrics_json` to a TypedDict where every optional scalar is `Field[float | None] | Field[Failed]` (a sum type via Literal status, e.g. `{'value': float | None, 'status': Literal['ok','error','insufficient']}`). The TypedDict is overkill for now; the dict[str, str] map is the right size for this PR's scope.

title: `r_squared_status` is typed as plain `str` in the return signature — drops Literal/Enum exhaustiveness checking
summary: `compute_qstats_scalars` returns `dict[str, float | None | str]` (L791). The `str` widening exists ONLY to accommodate `r_squared_status`'s three-valued enum `'no_benchmark' | 'ok' | 'error'` (L807, L824, L836, L854, L856). The actual value space is closed and finite, but the type signature says any str is valid. A typo (`'er ror'`, `'No Benchmark'`, `'unknown'`) at any of the three assignment sites would pass static checks and only surface when a consumer's switch statement misses the case. The H-0718 docstring explicitly documents the three states; the code does not enforce them. Compounding: the `r_squared` finding pattern is exactly the kind of discriminated union that benefits MOST from Literal — consumers should be able to switch on status and have the type checker verify exhaustiveness. Today they cannot.
evidence: metrics.py:7 `from typing import Any, TypedDict` (Literal not imported). metrics.py:791 `dict[str, float | None | str]`. metrics.py:807 docstring lists the 3 values. metrics.py:836,854,856 — 3 string-literal assignment sites that could drift.
fix: Import `Literal` and define `RSquaredStatus = Literal['no_benchmark', 'ok', 'error']` at module scope. Either (a) define a TypedDict `QstatsScalarsResult` with `r_squared_status: RSquaredStatus`, dropping the `| str` from the dict value type (compute_qstats_scalars return becomes `dict[str, float | None | RSquaredStatus]` which is still loose but at least pins the enum), or (b) leave the dict-of-Any but annotate the 3 assignment sites: `result['r_squared_status'] = cast(RSquaredStatus, 'ok')`. Option (a) catches typos at assignment time; option (b) is documentation-only. Recommend (a) — the Literal definition alone is 1 LoC and any future caller can switch on it with exhaustiveness checking.

title: compute_qstats_scalars return is `dict[str, float | None | str]` — every consumer must defensively narrow `str` to handle ONE key
summary: The function returns `dict[str, float | None | str]` (L791). 10 of 11 keys are `float | None`; only `r_squared_status` is `str`. Every consumer (test_metrics.py and any downstream code that reads this dict) must check `if isinstance(v, str): ... else: ...` to safely use a value, even though for 10/11 keys the str branch is dead. Worse: a consumer that wants r_squared the float can write `r_sq: float = result['r_squared']` and the type checker accepts it (the dict value is `float | None | str`, but Python's runtime hands back float | None for that key — the type widens through the dict erasure). This is the classic 'a TypedDict would be honest, a dict[str, Union] is dishonest' case. The sweep doesn't introduce this — it pre-existed — but the sweep ADDS to it (the new `*_error: str` keys on metrics_json widen the parent dict the same way).
evidence: metrics.py:826-838 initializer mixes float|None values with a str value under the same dict[str, float | None | str] umbrella. Consumers reading r_squared as a float lose the type checker's help.
fix: Define a TypedDict for the return: `class QstatsScalarsResult(TypedDict): recovery_factor: float | None; ulcer_index: float | None; ...; r_squared_status: RSquaredStatus; time_in_market: float | None`. Update the function signature to `-> QstatsScalarsResult`. Each field has a precise type. The 'returns is None' guard at L839 becomes irrelevant for typing (the signature requires returns: pd.Series, non-Optional). Estimated impact: ~25 LoC for the TypedDict definition, zero behavior change, type checker catches the next typo.

title: `*_error` companion keys written into metrics_json but no consumer reads them — the new type-level 'companion' contract is fiction
summary: The sweep writes `metrics_json['drawdown_episodes_error']`, `metrics_json['benchmark_metrics_error']`, `metrics_json['benchmark_returns_error']` to a Postgres JSONB column (`strategy_analytics.metrics_json`). The new comments justify these as 'so the frontend can render "Drawdown episodes unavailable due to compute error"'. Grep confirms ZERO consumers in `.ts` / `.tsx` (Next.js frontend) AND zero non-test consumers in Python. The `_error: str` keys are pure write-only: they bloat the JSONB payload by up to 200 chars × 3 keys per failure, and they encode a contract that no code reads. Type-design lens: the sweep introduces a 'companion error string' shape in the schema but no consumer is bound to it — the type contract is unobserved by any downstream type. A future engineer reading the code might trust the comment and depend on the key; on a JSONB payload that's already a typed-erased Dict[str, Any], that trust is brittle. Either remove the keys (the WARNING log already serves operator triage, redundant with the JSONB blob) or wire ONE actual consumer (e.g., a small React `<ErrorBanner metricKey='drawdown_episodes'/>` that reads metrics_json[`${metricKey}_error`]) so the contract becomes load-bearing.
evidence: `grep -rn 'drawdown_episodes_error\|benchmark_metrics_error\|benchmark_returns_error' --include='*.ts' --include='*.tsx'` returns zero hits. `grep -rn` across the whole repo finds them only in the metrics.py write sites and the changelog. The comments at L547, L606, L636 reference a frontend consumer that does not exist.
fix: Pick one: (a) remove the 3 `*_error` writes — the WARNING log + the absent metric key already disambiguate failure-vs-no-data for operators (Railway log query covers it); (b) wire ONE frontend consumer that reads the key to render an error banner so the contract is observed — this is the better long-term direction but out of scope for the silent-failure sweep. Recommendation: (a) for this PR (drops 3 lines), file a follow-up to add the frontend banner consumer in a separate UI PR.

title: `_safe_float(value: Any) -> float | None` conflates two distinct failure modes ('input not coercible' vs 'coerced but NaN/Inf') under the same None sentinel
summary: After the sweep, `_safe_float` now logs DEBUG for both failure modes — but the return type remains `float | None`. A caller cannot programmatically distinguish 'qs.stats returned a Decimal' (TypeError) from 'qs.stats returned np.nan' (in-band) by inspecting the return value. The red-team HIGH #1 chain identified this — the DEBUG logging closes the operator-triage gap, but the type-level information is still erased. For a true sum-type encoding the helper would return `float | None | UncoercibleReason` (or use `result.Ok/Err`). The current design is intentional (`float | None` is the legacy contract every site relies on) but worth flagging: the new DEBUG line is a band-aid on a type that doesn't carry the failure-mode discriminator. Not a regression — pre-sweep had the same blind spot — but the sweep made it visible by attempting to make _safe_float fail-loud at the LOG layer while leaving the TYPE layer unchanged.
evidence: metrics.py:172-202 — return type `float | None`. Two except clauses both return None; DEBUG distinguishes them in logs but not at the type level. Callers cannot branch on the failure mode.
fix: DEFER. Migrating _safe_float to a sum type touches every caller (every qs.stats wrapper site + sanitize_metrics + the rolling helpers). For this PR's scope the DEBUG logging is the right fix. Document as a long-tail follow-up: 'if/when introducing a Result[float, CoerceError] type for analytics, _safe_float is the entry point'.

title: `compute_qstats_scalars` declares `returns_len: int | None` to its helper but the outer function makes the None branch unreachable
summary: `_safe_qstats_scalar(name, fn, returns, returns_len: int | None)` accepts an int-or-None for the log helper. compute_qstats_scalars computes `returns_len = len(returns) if returns is not None else None` at L839 — same defensive-against-None pattern as compute_all_metrics' `returns_len_for_log` (already flagged LOW). Same root issue: `returns: pd.Series` is non-Optional per signature; the None branch never triggers. The implicit type `int | None` propagates through the helper and forces it to format `%s` not `%d` (which it correctly does at L102-103). Not broken — just unnecessarily wide. The signature could be `returns_len: int` since returns is non-Optional in the caller; that would type-narrow the log formatting.
evidence: metrics.py:91 `returns_len: int | None`. metrics.py:839 same conditional as compute_all_metrics. Both functions are typed `returns: pd.Series` (non-Optional).
fix: Drop the `| None` from both `_safe_qstats_scalar`'s signature and the conditional in compute_qstats_scalars / compute_all_metrics. Type-narrowing only; behavior unchanged. Cheap (3 lines).

title: FillRow TypedDict declares 16 keys but `daily_pnl` items written in fetch_daily_pnl use a DIFFERENT 9-key ad-hoc dict — two parallel row shapes for the same target table
summary: `exchange.py` defines `FillRow` (L55-79) as the typed shape for normalized fill rows written to `trades`. Every entry built via `_make_fill_dict` (L88-...) is FillRow-shaped. But `fetch_daily_pnl` builds ad-hoc 9-key dicts (L510-521, L536-546, L566-573, L598-608) typed loosely as `dict[str, Any]` and returned as `list[dict[str, Any]]`. Both shapes target the SAME trades table (or its daily_pnl equivalent). The sweep does not touch this — but it touches three sites (Bybit, Binance both branches) that build the loosely-typed dict. A type-design opportunity that the sweep declined: harmonize the daily-pnl row builders into a typed `DailyPnlRow(TypedDict)` so a missing key (`fee_currency`, `order_type`) fails at construction-time, not at JSONB-upsert-time when a downstream consumer hits KeyError. Pre-sweep this risk existed; post-sweep the sweep documented behavior but did not improve the type contract — invariant enforcement remains 'eyeball the dict literal'.
evidence: exchange.py:55-79 FillRow definition + _make_fill_dict factory. Compare to L510-521 (OKX), L536-546 (Binance futures-income), L566-573 (Binance fallback), L598-608 (Bybit) — four near-identical 9-key dict literals, all built ad-hoc, all returned via `list[dict[str, Any]]`.
fix: Define `class DailyPnlRow(TypedDict): exchange: str; symbol: str; side: Literal['buy','sell']; price: float; quantity: int; fee: float; fee_currency: str; timestamp: str; order_type: str` next to FillRow (~10 lines). Change fetch_daily_pnl signature to `-> list[DailyPnlRow]`. Each of the four call sites becomes a typed dict literal that mypy/pyright will validate. DEFERRED (out of this PR's scope) but flag in follow-ups.md.
